title: Lambda expressions
analysis: This task adds lambda/closure expressions (`|x| x * 2`) to the Reify language pipeline. The implementation spans 4 layers: grammar (tree-sitter), syntax AST (reify-syntax), compiled IR (reify-types/expr.rs + reify-compiler), and evaluation (reify-expr). Key design considerations: (1) The `|` delimiter for lambda params must not conflict with `||` (logical OR) — tree-sitter handles via longest-match but we may need a `conflicts` entry. (2) Lambda params have optional type annotations (`|x: Real| ...`). (3) Closures capture free variables from the enclosing CompilationScope — during compilation we analyze the body for identifiers that resolve in the outer scope but aren't lambda params, recording their ValueCellIds as captures. (4) At eval time, a lambda creates a Value::Lambda closure that snapshots the captured ValueCellId→Value mappings from the current ValueMap. (5) Type::Function already exists in reify-types. (6) Collection operations (map, filter, fold) are out of scope for this task — this task only adds lambda syntax, compilation, and evaluation. Lambda application will be tested via direct call or standalone eval. The compiler currently has placeholder stubs for collection literals (ListLiteral etc) that return Undef with diagnostics — lambda compilation follows a similar pattern but will be fully implemented. Content hashing needs a new tag byte (12) for Lambda values.

prerequisites.description: Add lambda_expression and lambda_param rules to tree-sitter-reify/grammar.js, then regenerate the parser with `tree-sitter generate`. Lambda syntax: `|params| body` where params are `identifier` or `identifier : type_expr`, comma-separated. Add lambda_expression to _expression choice (not _primary_expression) to give it proper precedence. Must handle `|` vs `||` — add to conflicts if needed.
Add ExprKind::Lambda { params: Vec<LambdaParam>, body: Box<Expr> } and LambdaParam { name: String, type_expr: Option<TypeExpr>, span: SourceSpan } to reify-syntax/src/lib.rs AST types.

Add CompiledExprKind::Lambda { params: Vec<(String, Option<Type>)>, body: Box<CompiledExpr>, captures: Vec<ValueCellId> } to reify-types/src/expr.rs. Add a CompiledExpr::lambda() constructor with content_hash tag byte [7] (using a distinct tag). Also add Value::Lambda { params: Vec<String>, body: Box<CompiledExpr>, captures: ValueMap } to reify-types/src/value.rs with content_hash tag byte [12], Display, PartialEq, Eq, Ord implementations.
Update reify-types/src/lib.rs to export the new types (if LambdaParam etc are placed there). Ensure CompiledExprKind::Lambda and Value::Lambda are visible to downstream crates.

steps.description: Write failing parser test: parse `|x| x * 2` as a lambda_expression node in tree-sitter, verify no parse errors. Add test in crates/reify-syntax/tests/lambda_tests.rs.

Add lower_lambda_expression() to ts_parser.rs: handle 'lambda_expression' node kind by extracting lambda_param children (name + optional type_expr) and body expression. Wire it into lower_expr() dispatch. This should make step-1 pass.
Write failing parser test: parse `|x: Real, y: Real| x + y` → Lambda with 2 typed params. Verify param names and type annotations are correctly extracted.
Ensure typed lambda params are correctly lowered — the optional `: type_expr` part in lambda_param should produce LambdaParam with type_expr: Some(TypeExpr). Make step-3 pass.
Write failing parser test: parse `|| true` (zero-param lambda) and `|x, y, z| x + y + z` (3-param untyped lambda). Verify edge cases.
Handle zero-param and multi-param lambda edge cases in the parser lowering. Make step-5 pass.
Write failing compiler test: compile a lambda `|x| x * 2` in a let binding context. Verify it produces CompiledExprKind::Lambda with 1 param, no captures, and correct body.

Add ExprKind::Lambda handling to compile_expr_guarded() in reify-compiler/src/lib.rs. Create a nested CompilationScope for the lambda body with lambda params registered. Compile the body expression in that nested scope. Initially, captures = empty vec. Result type is Type::Function. Make step-7 pass.
Write failing compiler test: compile `let factor = 3; |x| x * factor` — verify that the lambda's captures vec contains the ValueCellId for 'factor'.

Implement capture analysis: after compiling the lambda body, walk the CompiledExpr tree collecting all ValueRef(id) references. Filter to only those ids that come from the outer scope (not lambda params). Store as captures in CompiledExprKind::Lambda. Make step-9 pass.
Write failing compiler test: compile lambda with typed params `|x: Real| x + 1.0` — verify param type is recorded as Some(Type::Real) in the compiled output.
Handle typed lambda params in the compiler: resolve type name to Type (using existing type resolution logic). Make step-11 pass.
Write failing eval test: evaluate a lambda expression `|x| x * 2` — verify it produces Value::Lambda with the correct params and captured values.

Add CompiledExprKind::Lambda handling to eval_expr() in reify-expr/src/lib.rs. When evaluating a lambda expression, snapshot the captured ValueCellId values from the current ValueMap into a new ValueMap, then return Value::Lambda { params, body, captures }. Make step-13 pass.
Write failing eval test: evaluate a lambda with captures — `factor=3` in ValueMap, eval `|x| x * factor`. Verify the resulting Value::Lambda captures the factor value from the ValueMap.
Ensure capture evaluation correctly snapshots outer scope values. Make step-15 pass.
Write failing eval test: evaluate a lambda with Undef capture — one captured variable is Undef. Verify the lambda is still created but the capture contains Undef.
Ensure Undef propagation in lambda capture: if a captured value is Undef, it's stored as Undef in the closure (lazy Undef propagation). Make step-17 pass.
Write failing eval test: call/apply a Value::Lambda — create a helper function `apply_lambda(lambda: &Value, args: &[Value]) -> Value` and test `(|x| x * 2)` applied to `[Int(5)]` returns `Int(10)`.

Implement apply_lambda() in reify-expr: given a Value::Lambda and argument values, create a ValueMap with params bound to args plus captures, evaluate the body. Export the function. Make step-19 pass.
Write failing eval test: apply a lambda with captures — `factor=3`, lambda `|x| x * factor`, apply to `[Int(5)]` returns `Int(15)`.
Ensure apply_lambda merges capture ValueMap with param bindings. Make step-21 pass.
Write failing eval test: apply a lambda with wrong arity (2-param lambda applied with 1 arg) returns Undef. Also test 0-param lambda application.
Add arity check to apply_lambda — if args count != params count, return Undef. Make step-23 pass.
Write test: Value::Lambda content_hash is deterministic and distinct from other Value variants. Two identical lambdas produce the same hash; different lambdas produce different hashes.
Ensure Value::Lambda content_hash, Display, PartialEq, Eq, Ord are correct. Make step-25 pass.

Write integration test: full pipeline parse → compile → eval for `structure S { let factor: Real = 3; }` with lambda `|x| x * factor` compiled in that scope, then applied. (May be simplified to a compiler round-trip test.)
Fix any integration issues found in step-27. Ensure all tests pass. Run full test suite to verify no regressions.

Write failing test in crates/reify-expr/tests/lambda_eval_tests.rs (or crates/reify-types/src/value.rs unit tests): create two Value::Lambda instances with identical params and body but with captures inserted into the ValueMap in different orders (e.g., lambda_a inserts capture 'x' then 'y', lambda_b inserts 'y' then 'x'). Assert that (1) lambda_a == lambda_b (PartialEq, which sorts — should pass), and (2) lambda_a.content_hash() == lambda_b.content_hash() (this MUST fail with the current code, demonstrating the hash invariant violation). This directly tests the invariant `a == b → a.content_hash() == b.content_hash()`.

Fix Value::Lambda content_hash() in crates/reify-types/src/value.rs: before hashing captures, collect the (id, val) pairs from captures.iter() into a Vec, sort by key using sort_by_key(|(id, _)| format!("{}", id)) (matching the PartialEq approach), then iterate the sorted vec to build the hash. This ensures hash iteration order is deterministic regardless of im::HashMap internal ordering, restoring the invariant `a == b → a.content_hash() == b.content_hash()`. Make step-29 pass. Run the full test suite to verify no regressions.

design_decisions.rationale: Lambda has its own precedence behavior — the body extends as far right as possible (lowest precedence on the right). Placing it in _expression ensures `|x| x * 2 + 3` parses as `|x| ((x * 2) + 3)` not `(|x| x * 2) + 3`. Using prec.right(0) allows the body to capture the full right-hand expression.

design_decisions.decision: Value::Lambda stores a Box<CompiledExpr> body and a ValueMap of captures, not raw closures
design_decisions.rationale: The evaluator is a pure recursive tree walker. Storing the compiled expression body + captured values allows re-evaluation of the lambda body in apply_lambda(). This is consistent with the existing evaluation model where everything is data (no Rust closures in Values).

design_decisions.decision: Capture analysis happens at compile time by walking the compiled body's ValueRef nodes
design_decisions.rationale: The compiler already resolves all identifiers to ValueCellIds. After compiling the lambda body in a nested scope (where lambda params are registered as fresh ValueCellIds), any ValueRef in the body that refers to an outer scope's ValueCellId is a capture. This is simpler and more reliable than AST-level free-variable analysis.

design_decisions.decision: apply_lambda is a standalone function in reify-expr, not a new CompiledExprKind variant
design_decisions.rationale: Lambda application will be driven by collection operations (map, filter, fold — separate task) or future direct-call syntax. Adding a separate `apply_lambda` function keeps the core eval_expr clean and allows collection operations to call it without adding a new expression kind for 'lambda call'.

design_decisions.decision: Lambda params get synthetic ValueCellIds (e.g., '$lambda.x') during compilation
design_decisions.rationale: The CompilationScope maps names to ValueCellIds. Lambda params need unique ValueCellIds that won't collide with the enclosing scope's names. Using a '$lambda' prefix (or similar) ensures uniqueness. At eval time, apply_lambda maps these synthetic ids to the actual argument values.
Value content hash tags use 0-11 currently (0=Bool through 11=Option, with 10 reserved for Satisfaction). Tag 12 is the next available and avoids collision with all existing tags.

design_decisions.decision: Type inference deferred — lambda result type is Type::Function with param types from annotations or Type::Real default
design_decisions.rationale: Full type inference for lambda params from call-site context (e.g., `.map(|x| x + 1)` inferring x as the list element type) requires integration with collection operations, which is a separate task. For now, untyped lambda params default to Type::Real, which covers the most common CAD use case. This will be refined when collection operations are implemented.

design_decisions.decision: Sort captures by key before hashing in content_hash() to match PartialEq behavior
design_decisions.rationale: ValueMap is backed by im::HashMap which has non-deterministic iteration order. PartialEq already sorts captures before comparing (lines 186-195), documenting awareness of the ordering issue. content_hash() must do the same sort to maintain the mandatory invariant `a == b → a.content_hash() == b.content_hash()`. Without this, the incremental evaluation cache can be corrupted by spurious hash mismatches for logically equal lambda values.

reuse.how: Use for comma-separated lambda_param list: commaSep($.lambda_param)
Clone the outer scope and register lambda params in the clone, then compile the body with the cloned scope. This mirrors how function body compilation works.
Add a new ExprKind::Lambda match arm following the same pattern as other expression kinds.

reuse.what: CompiledExpr builder constructors (literal, value_ref, binop, unop)
reuse.how: Add a CompiledExpr::lambda() constructor following the same pattern with content_hash computation.
Use tag [12] for Value::Lambda, following the same pattern as other variants.
Add CompiledExprKind::Lambda match arm to create a Value::Lambda with captured values from the ValueMap.
Use Type::Function { params, return_type } as the result_type for compiled lambda expressions.
ValueMap uses PersistentMap (im::HashMap) with O(1) clone — perfect for storing captured values in closures.
Follow the same parse-and-assert pattern for lambda parsing tests. Create a parse_expr helper that wraps the expression in a structure let binding.
Apply the same sort_by_key(|(id, _)| format!("{}", id)) pattern in content_hash() before iterating captures for hashing.
